sentences.text: I looked down this--
A month later, like I'm telling, again, same trucks come around and were stopping at the same place where they picked up the first 400 people.
This time, they picked up woman and child--
--and children.
And they, also, a couple hundred, they took them out in a little forest out of town to execute it, always the night, the same thing.
And--
No, also, I just remember I was in the house and somebody said, let's go into this house.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And I-- I was-- let's go in this house.
So the commander said, we were in this house.
We took out already.
There's nobody there.
So he passed over our house.
[SIGHS] After this, they started dig graves near the brick house where used to make bricks.
As so we went to the leaders from the ghetto.
And because was rumors this would be digging the graves for ourself.
So we sent the leaders from our ghetto to the [INAUDIBLE] commissar.
And he did the opposite.
He said if you put out rumors like this, I will punish you.
[INAUDIBLE] Nobody will touch [INAUDIBLE].
We need you for work.
And that's why we dig the-- the-- the graves there, were because we've going to build another brick house.
We're going to expand it.
And false rumors will be punished by death.
And he put a contribution for-- for because [INAUDIBLE] and we're supposed to bring so much and so much gold and bring it in.
Every time come out different laws.
People have to bring in their gold, bring in their furniture.
They bring in the-- the food.
You weren't allowed to have five pound food, just very drastic laws, terrible and terrible more from-- from day-to-day.
I start to wear the blue arm.
They give us to wear patch, one in the front and one in the back, about three inches yellow patch.
And every time a [INAUDIBLE] was they give orders, always they made us in the holidays.
The holidays.
Always in the holidays.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Like-- like they're fighting with the-- with guard.
You asked me before what did my people-- my parents believed and everything.
Nobody could believe things like this can happen.
Before the war, at least when a pogrom-- a pogrom or something, we used to run to dignitaries and begging for help and everything.
Here, this time we went to them.
The answer was, God punish you.
You sinners, and God punish you.
And then we want to vent to the Jewish leaders and to the religious rabbis asking for question, why it's happened to us?
Why should I have some suffer?
Why is the righteous people suffering, et cetera?
So they answer, [INAUDIBLE].
The righteous suffer.
They have to suffer for the better of people, always have those excuses.
[SIGHS] ?]
But the meantime, in the town people see is not well and nothing will help.
People were talking to escape to the forest because the Germans used to kill the prisoners of war.
These prisoners of war escaped.
Instead, to go in prison, they rather escape then live in the forest.
They start organized already resistance by little groups, Jewish people too.
Actually, being in our ghetto, we tried to accumulate weapon and money and to be organized around the forest.
But this was before when the people said, where are we going?
Die in-- in the forest in the winter?
May as well we'll die in the same in the ghetto.
I worked on a farm.
And my mother worked on farm.
This was a farm was a-- but the-- the girl was a lover with a [INAUDIBLE] commissar.
And he took away cows, wagons, horses, and everything.
He rebuilt their-- collect their farm.
Because when the Russians came in, they took it away from this good [INAUDIBLE], took away farm and give him back.
And he owed my family a favor because the-- when the Bolsheviks came in, they sold my father a big farm.
And then when the Bolsheviks retreat, my father give him back.
So we worked on his farm.
And this way, we could support ourself to live in the ghetto too.
So when they used to be here catching, to catch people to execute or labor or something, I used to sleep over in the farm.
But one day, my uncle, my mother's brother, came over to me.
He was a military man.
He wasn't a Polish man.
He knew weapons.
He knew how to handle.
Came in to me, and he told me that he's preparing for himself to go away in the partisans.
So I begged him, why can't you take me with you?
I said, first of all, you're too little.
Second, you have to have a weapon.
And without weapon, you cannot-- we can't-- can't take you.
They wouldn't accept you there.
So I surprised him.
I tell him, you know what?
I have a weapon.
What happens if I give you a weapon?
Yes, he was surprised.
So he agreed I should bring him the weapon.

I forgot to tell you before that the weapon what I hide in the building near me in the ghetto, I don't know who guide me, but I was afraid they will find a weapon in the ghetto, they may blame the whole ghetto or something.
So I smuggled this out, out of town in a little mountain [INAUDIBLE] down there, and a big tree with I used to play there, climbing the tree under the-- between the roots.
I dig the hole.
I took the weapon in a can with grease.
And I digged it down there, are I hide it there.
But now, I had a problem, how to get this back.
When I-- when I put the-- the weapon on the mountain there, it was a part on the ghetto.
And then when it was a year later, this was out of the ghetto.
So I had a problem how to get this back.
So I was a shepherd on this farm.
So I used to take the cows closer to this mountain.
If they ask me, I would say, the-- the cows ran away and I'm going to bring the cows back.
So I sneaked.
It was close to this [INAUDIBLE].
And I took out the weapon.
And I smuggled the same back to the ghetto.
And I gave it to my uncle.
And October 1st, 1942, this farmer, the head of the farm what the owner find out from this [INAUDIBLE] commissar, this is the last day from the ghetto.
He want-- he went.
He told my mother.
And my mother went to the leaders from the ghetto to warn them.
And they went.
They deny it for their leader.
But we know the truth already.
So I went out and made arrangement with my uncle to meet.
If it happened, we have it to go leave together.
So my mother gave me all kind of possessions, gold, watches, leather, highland, [INAUDIBLE] packed up for me.
We-- and she told me to go to my uncle, to go away to the forest from this.
And I'm separated from my mother, my sister, my little bothers-- [CRYING] brothers.
So it was the last time I saw them.
And I went to my uncle.
I went to my uncle.
So I told him we have to go.
So he said, I have to make a-- he has to make arrangement, something with the wife, with the kids and everything.
I should go in this-- this place by the post office out of town.
There we meet.
He knew where to meet there.
And also, my older cousins from the-- my mother's side's supposed to meet there and coming.
This was already dark, night time.
I was alone.
Was some people there, about 20 people or something, maybe more.
And all of a sudden, rumors came that the gestapo's on the way here.
So we left.
We left.
And we went out of town.
Went out of town, we saw the Ukrainian police. when the villagers come to help to liquidate the ghetto.
And we-- well, right until the police passed, then we went to the forest.
We supposed to meet the other groups.
We never met.
Was other groups was to meet to go together to the partisans.
We never met.
So we went to the partisans.
And first time we reached, we reached one guy.
He didn't want to give us guidance.
We threaten him.
He give us food.
And then they start shooting.
The people disappeared.
We couldn't find them anymore.
And in the older group, we left.
We were about six people, three, and three-- two girls and another man.
And we were going deep in the forest, going going deep in the forest.
Meantime, we were hungry, so decided to send in the girls to beg for food.
We figured girls, maybe they'll feel sorry.
The farmers will give them-- men, they will be suspicious.
It was a mistake.
They never let the girls out, and we never saw them again.
And then we went again for-- we caught one farmer.
And he want to give us out.
We threaten him with a gun.
We had still the gun there.
We threaten him.
And he finally give us some food.
And those-- the other two people, and one, again, escaped because he knew a farmer, went to hide by a farmer.
So we left three people, me, whatever was smallest, and there are other people were 20 years old or 22.
One was from the town of Warsaw.
And they decide to go back their native village and to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hide out by farmers.
They said they knew good farmers that will hide out with them.
But they didn't-- they didn't tell me that I can go with them.
But I had my own plans already, because I told you when I worked by the railroad, I know the train goes to the left-- Good.
--is Russia.
To the right is Germany.
And I want to go closer to the front to save myself.
Was already November, very cold, you know, month of November, very cold.
We came back to the town.
And I was-- I met-- I was hiding out by the farmer, night time.
And under the barn-- I crawled under-- I find another Jew.
The name was Pini Plevy.
And he told me that he wants to go, also, to the forest, so farmer to hide himself out.
And he will take me.
But the meantime, I was going-- I crawled out.
I was going around begging for food.
And I couldn't find any food.
And I see-- I heard shooting, the lighting, because I figured they go after me.
So I crawled in, in the barn, and I hide myself out below in hay in straw.
I way laying.
And all day, I was laying there.
And I heard the Germans and everybody is already celebrating with the goods from the Jewish homes.
This belongs to me.
This belongs to them and everything.
And I want praying nobody should come in take hay, because in this place we were sleeping there when we working.
And he used to come and punch in for to take out hay.
Finally, till became nighttime, dark, I crawled out.
And I went to the servants from this rich farmer.
And they knew me.
And they-- they told me I want some possessions.
But yeah, they had them hidden.
So she said, no, my mother came and she gave it to him.
So I said, give me something to eat.
So she said to wait.
And I-- I should be outside in the outdoor, you know, facilities.
Outhouse.
Outhouse facilities.
So I was staying there.
I'm lucky she didn't turn me away with the Germans.
She really brought me out a half a loaf of bread with pork.
And this-- I-- I didn't-- we didn't eat pork, you know?
But this I had no choice.
I had-- I had to eat pork.
And-- and I went through the fields.
And the other side was the railroad station to catch train to go to the front.
In the meantime, it's nighttime.
And I hear shooting, lights, and everything.
They didn't-- they didn't mean to me, just was like to shooting.
But I-- I was afraid she gave me out.
But it wasn't true.
She didn't give me out.
So I was running, running.
Then I came to the railroad station.
And the train came in from it.
And I slowed down.
I jumped on the train.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] I jumped on the train, and I came following town.
I came to a little town, [POLISH].
The conductors went checking the-- the trains and everything.
They saw me.
They start yelling, so I jumped out.
They were running after me.
All of a sudden the train take off.
I jumped in another wagon, and I took off.
I came to a second city in the morning.
Carol, in the morning, I came to second city, the town of Kovel.
There's a big railroad station.
And to my surprise, they opened the cargo trains, was-- the cargo was human, was volunteer workers from Ukraine or Russia.
But they worked in Germany.
They let them go home.
Or they worked there to finish their term, or they were sick and everything.
So I heard-- I heard their stories from what town, where to work in Germany.
So I became one of them, that now I a Ukrainian.
I came back from work from Germany, and I picked up a one triangle what they're wearing.
I put this on me.
And I was with a group of them.
They took another train.
I go back to the train.
I came to the town of Zdolbuniv.
In town of Zdolbuniv, I was hungry.
I was going out begging for food near the railroad station.
So the guy, a gentile, I ask him to eat.
I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
I said here, you have tomatoes on the window.
Give me a tomato.
He said, they're not good.
They're green.
I said, I'll keep them till they're red.
And he said, they're not good for you, he said.
But go across the street.
Those are good people.
They'll give it to you.
He was suspicious.
He knew quite I'm Jewish.
So I went across the street.
I knocked the door.
It's a German gendarme, shaves himself.
And he asked me what I want.
I'm sent.
But I make believe I don't know anything.
Said the wrong-- the wrong word [INAUDIBLE].
I walked out.
And he's-- didn't follow me.
I run over to the railroad station.
And first cargo train, I went up.
It was other people, you know, from this train, was in Germany.
And Germans was sitting on the train.
I went over to the Germans begging food.
They gave me food.
They give me cigarettes.
They didn't know anything.
And I continued till I come from the town Kazatin.
I came in midnight.
I don't know where to go, who to go.
I'm cold.
I'm shivering.
And-- and-- and yeah, when I was riding this railroad station nighttime, so I went to the conductor, conductor, what he-- you know, that runs the train?
And I asked him should give me permission to-- I'll help him there, start to draw wood in the engine, coal in the engine.
And he gave me water to drink and everything.
So until I came in the-- in midnight, I came into the town of Kazatin.
I just went a little bridge.
They call this a bridge, you know, like maybe it be from here to here.
And the space underneath is that much space.
I went underneath, sit down in the dirt where people used to go make whatever they have to make.
And whole night sitting there shivering.
And I put my arms one into here, and one into here, and then my-- my head in the middle, and keep myself warm until early in the morning.
Then early in the morning, then I went to begging for food.
And then I looked for directions to go to the front.
That was a very big railroad station with maybe hundreds directions.
At Central Station they-- the railroads go different places.
And so I was set to go for Kiev.
I went-- I went with this east.
So I wind up south.
So I came to this grand town of Vinnitsa.
I came in town of the Vinnitsa.
There I was walking in the street.
The lady-- you remember I told you the story of [INAUDIBLE]?
When the Polish army came back, the lady what I was asked the priest not to do harm to us?
I saw her there.
All of a sudden, she's the middle of the street.
And she jumped at me.
And she start to hug me and kiss me.
And said, Abraham, Abraham.
I said, don't say Abraham, say anything.
My name is Andre.
And she recognized me.
And then-- but this woman was a delicate lady.
In our town, she was but maybe 4 feet 5', little.
And all of a sudden here, she was skinny.
She was in the black market, marketing here.
Because her husband died, she moved to her sister.
She had a sister in Stan.
And she was on the black market.
OK.
Then I ask her, maybe I can stay with you.
She said, no.
You cannot stay with me.
My sister is very anti-Semitic, and she will give you out.
And she start to tell me stories.
You go to-- to Zhmerinka, and-- and the other side from the river there is Jewish people in Jewish ghetto.
From excitement, I didn't know too much.
So I didn't know to ask her where is Zhmerinka, how to go and everything.
She disappeared in this night,
I went back to this town of Kazatin the first time when I came in.
And I figured maybe there, I'll still look for Kiev.
In this direction, I know Kiev has the front, has to be at the Kiev.
So I couldn't find it.
So all day, wandering, wandering, till I got disgusted.
I go over and see a train station and people in the train.
So I go over to the German SS man.
What happened with those people in the train?
He said, they volunteered to go to Germany to work.
Hm.
I said, can I go in?
He said yes.
Go ahead.
So I said-- so I'm going in the train.
In the meantime, those people, the went for shopping in the street, in the bazaar for different thing.
So I was alone, sitting in the train.
They come back.
They see me there.
They're saying, what are you doing here?
I said, I'm going to Germany.
They go-- they go out.
And they're going to the German and said, they said, he's not going to Germany to work.
His a thief.
He always were-- he wants to steal our luggage.
And then he will go off to second station.
Germans take me off and start hitting me and beating me up.
And I was yelling and this.
I don't know.
I escaped.
I escaped.
And I have to go.
Where should I go?
I don't know where to go.
Finally, It's almost night.
Where am I going to sleep?
I almost froze to death yesternight.
So I'm going to the main railroad station.
The main railroad station, I come to the station, two police standing by the door and checking documents.
I didn't have any documents, anything.
So I wait till a lot of people together push themself in to go in.
And this winter's cold.
I'm between-- I'm between their legs.
I push myself in.
I went into the-- this station.
In the station, hundreds of people sleeping on the floor, in the hallway, you know, like Grand Central Station.
So I pushed myself up all the way in the top.
OK, they're going around checking the documents.
I figured on the top, the police wouldn't step on people.
So I pushed my way up the way on this top.
And I find a spare-- a place no one was sleeping.
And meantime, I went out and was a big restaurant.
I went into the Germans, all kind of Nazi Germans army and begging food for the table.
They're laughing, but throwing me a bone.
What ever is said-- yeah, yeah, yeah-- I said, yeah, yeah.
Just-- I didn't understood.
But all they had answers, yeah, yeah.
They-- they bless me, I said yeah.
They cursed me, I said yeah.
And the food-- I slept over all night.
The following morning, breakfast, I went out again begging for food.
And then I have to find out how to get out.
You get out.
They're checking you, also, the documents.
So I had to wait the same thing.
Not when then, when a lot of people get together will try to push themselves out.
A lot of people, in between I sneaked out.
I don't know what chance I took.
And-- so I didn't know where to go, so I figured I'd go back to Vinnitsa to meet this woman.
Maybe she will explain me more this.
So I'm went by railway, went back there.
I went back to this town of Vinnitsa.
All of a sudden I got acquainted with street gangs, street gangs, you know, for stealing, begging, and this.
And they slept in the old broken stores.
And they weren't suspicious.
There know my name is Andre.
And that's what [INAUDIBLE].
One guy one day comes in to me and said to me, is you Jewish?
I said, no.
Why-- why would you make you to think I'm Jewish?
Don't be afraid.
I'm half Jewish.
So I was thinking to me, I never heard of such a thing, half Jewish.
Oh, you're Jewish, or you're not Jewish.
I never heard, from my time, I never heard of thing.
I didn't tell him this, but I-- I wouldn't admit.
So you didn't pay attention even-- but his friends, probably they were talking over, one day in the morning to bringing in the police to me, to bring in police to me.
And they tell me, you're Jewish?
I said no, the policeman.
They said, say 'kukurydza.' 'Kukurydza' because the Russian Jews, they talk with the throat.
Oh,
Not that the tongue.
So I say it correct, 'kukurydza,' [TRILLS AN 'R'] Now, they say, say 'orzel.' 'Kukurydza' means popcorn, and 'orzel' means eagle.
Say 'orzel.' I start to say [INAUDIBLE] I say, 'orzel.' He said no.
He tell me to put down my pants.
[INAUDIBLE] pants, those lady from my town, she advised me.
But whatever possession I had before, she sold it from black market.
And she bought me a watch.
She said with the watch, if a Ukraine is karbovanets, and Romania is liras, and Russia is rubles, so with the watch, you always can sell it and get the currency, the right currency.
So I took her advice.
And she really did.
I don't know if she made profit or not, but she used to sell for me to sell thing.
She bought me a watch.
This watch saved me, because the policeman took up [INAUDIBLE] watch was like possessions, like millions.
But nobody had watches during the [INAUDIBLE].
So he start playing with the-- with the arrows.
They fall down in the store.
He was looking for the arrow.
As I'm start to putting down the pants, I put up the pants and I jumped the window.
And I escaped.
Yeah, And I escaped.
I escaped.
I go and seem out of town.
I'm running, and those guys, what they gave me out, they were following me.
And they come on, come on, Andre.
Forget about something.
We were joking just.
I said, so what do you have in mind?
I said, we go and we go out of town.
We can make a job something.
We have to steal.
So we go out of town to see laundry drying outside town.
So I-- they said to me, we'll go into back, begging for food.
And you, Andre, you take the laundry.
I said, no.
I'll- I'm an expert in begging.
I don't know how to steal this-- this.
No, I'm expert in stealing.
You know, I never begging for bread till this.
Maybe you go in, and you too, they wouldn't be suspicious.
